title: Rat control on a smallholding
subtitle: Identifying the problem, removing the food source, and keeping numbers down.
excerpt: Any smallholding with livestock feed, an egg supply, and warm buildings will attract rats. They spoil feed, spread disease, and kill young poultry. A practical approach: identifying signs, eliminating harbourage, locking away feed, and managing bait stations consistently.
sourceNotes: British Pest Control Association (BPCA), rodent control guidance for agricultural premises. DEFRA/Natural England, rodenticide best practice advice. Carla Emery, Encyclopedia of Country Living (2012 edition), pest management chapter. HSE guidance on safe use of rodenticide bait.

glossaryTerms.definition: Any location where rats can nest, hide, or shelter undisturbed: gaps in walls, under concrete slabs, in stacked bales, inside compost heaps, or behind stored equipment. Removing harbourage is the first and most effective control measure.
A tamper-proof plastic box containing rodenticide bait. Designed so rats enter from both ends but children, dogs, and non-target wildlife cannot access the bait. Used outdoors and under cover.

content.text: Rats are a permanent fixture on any site with livestock. The goal is not elimination but management: keeping populations below the threshold where they cause serious damage or disease risk. Two interventions move the needle more than anything else: removing
harbourage
and securing food. Bait stations are the third layer, and they work far better when the first two are already in place.
Identifying a rat problem
Droppings: dark, cylindrical, 15 to 20 mm long, tapered at both ends. Concentrated runs along walls and under feed bins
Gnaw marks on feed bags, timber, and plastic water pipes
Burrows against walls and under concrete, typically 5 to 8 cm diameter
Smear marks along wall junctions where rats repeatedly run the same route
Missing or damaged young poultry, eggs, or rabbit kits
Removing harbourage

Walk around every building on the holding and note every potential nest site. Stacked bales against a wall create a perfect rat city; store them on pallets or on raised timber bearers. Remove any piles of timber, scrap metal, or equipment that have not moved in a year. Block gaps in walls and under doors with wire mesh (at least 0.5 cm x 0.5 cm, smaller than a rat's head). Rats can squeeze through a 15 mm gap.
Compost heaps are a rat magnet. If you are composting livestock manure or kitchen waste near the buildings, keep the heap turning regularly and do not add cooked food.
Securing food

Every bag of livestock feed left open overnight will be visited. Store all concentrate feed, hay pellets, and grain in metal galvanised bins with lids that latch. Metal is rat-proof; plastic bags and wooden boxes are not. Treadle feeders for poultry reduce the overnight food supply significantly; lift hanging feeders after dark.
Collect eggs daily, morning and evening. Eggs left in nesting boxes overnight attract rats and teach them that the poultry area is a consistent food source.
Bait stations
Once harbourage and food are reduced, place tamper-proof
bait stations

on the runs identified by droppings and smear marks. Position stations against walls with both entry holes flush to the wall. Rats follow wall lines and will not enter a station placed in the open. Use a second-generation anticoagulant rodenticide labelled for agricultural use. Follow the label instructions for bait quantity and replacement interval.

Check stations every 5 to 7 days. Replace consumed bait. A station that has not been touched in 3 weeks means rats are not using that run; move it to an active area. Remove all bait and stations once activity has stopped for 4 weeks.

attrs.title: Secondary poisoning risk
attrs.body: Second-generation anticoagulant rodenticides can kill raptors and predatory mammals that eat poisoned rats. Barn owls, buzzards, red kites, stoats, and ferrets are all at risk. Place bait stations where non-target animals cannot access the dead rats while they are still dying; keep cats and working dogs away from baited areas. The risk is manageable but not zero: use the minimum bait needed and do not leave active stations in place longer than necessary.

content.text: Ongoing monitoring

Rat populations rebuild from adjacent land within weeks of a clear-out. Monthly checks of burrow activity and bait stations keep the cycle manageable. Autumn, when field rats move inside ahead of harvest, is the most active period; increase monitoring and response in September and October.

items.symptom: Bait being taken but population not decreasing
items.cause: Bait stations not covering all active runs, or population pressure from adjacent land is too high for baiting alone
items.fix: Map all active runs with droppings and smear evidence. Add stations to cover runs not currently baited. Consider employing a pest controller if the holding borders heavily infested farmland.

items.symptom: Predator (hawk, owl, or fox) found dead near the holding
items.cause: Secondary poisoning from consuming a rat that had recently taken bait
items.fix: Report dead raptors to the Predatory Bird Monitoring Scheme (PBMS). Review bait station placement to reduce the number of dying rats accessible to predators and scavengers.

items.symptom: Rats returning within days of a clearance
items.cause: Burrows on the holding connect to a network under adjacent hedgerow or woodland
items.fix: Persistent reinfestation from underground networks means surface harbourage removal alone is not enough. Combine it with a long-term baiting programme at the boundary. Gas fumigation of burrow systems is a professional procedure. Contact a pest controller for large networks.
